Content:
You are a bioinformatics R script expert. Write and execute R script to preprocess the omics expression matrix data according to the following plan.

# Workspace Information
- Workspace root: G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/demo/
- Tmp directory: G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/demo/tmp
- Scripts directory: G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/demo/scripts
- R scripts tools directory: G:/OmicsWorks/agent/rscript
- R-sharp scripts tools directory: G:/OmicsWorks/agent/gcmodeller
- Python scripts tools directory: G:/OmicsWorks/agent/python
- KEGG background data directory: G:/OmicsWorks/agent/data
- molecule biological annotation table: G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/metabolites.csv (758.29 KB)

# Research Topic
我正在探讨高铁饮食对感染艰难梭菌小鼠的肠道免疫反应、肠道菌群及代谢的影响，并揭示特定的肠道菌群及其代谢物在艰难梭菌感染（CDI）中的具体作用机制。研究希望阐明“饮食-肠道菌群-宿主”之间的相互作用是如何调节宿主对CDI的易感性及疾病严重程度的。研究主要通过以下几个步骤和实验方法展开：

构建动物模型与表型分析：
构建了小鼠艰难梭菌感染（CDI）模型，并将其分为接受高铁饮食（含400 mg/kg硫酸亚铁）处理组和对照组。
观察发现高铁饮食能够减轻CDI，其机制与降低艰难梭菌的致病性以及改变宿主肠道中性粒细胞的招募有关。

在我的样本分组中，high iron diet before为在感染前进行了高铁饮食干预的病原菌感染实验组，Standard (control)则是什么也没有感染的健康小鼠对照样本，Clostridium difficile infection则是什么也没做处理的感染病原菌的处理组。QC组的样本是用于评估实验数据采集稳定性的QC组样本

# Omics Datasets (1)
## Dataset 1: unknown
- Expression file: G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/expression.csv (476.33 KB)
- Sample info file: G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/sampleinfo.csv (964 B)
- Sample count: 18
- Molecule count: 2059
- Sample IDs: Fc16YH_CD1,  Fc16YH_CD2,  Fc16YH_CD3,  Fc16YH_CD4,  Fc16YH_CD5,  Fc16YH_CD6,  Fc16YH_FE1 and Fc16YH_FE2 etc

# Knowledge Base

microbiota-host' interactions modulating CDI susceptibility and severity.","disease_or_phenotype": "Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI) in a murine model","organism": "Mus musculus (mouse), Clostridioides difficile","tissue": "Cecum, colon, feces, colonic lamina propria, serum","key_genes_proteins": ["TcdA","TcdB","Fur (ferric uptake regulator)","Calprotectin (S100A8/S100A9)","Lipocalin-2 (LCN2)","FeoA","FeoB","FeoB1","ZupT","Fhu operon (putative siderophore uptake)","HatR","HatT","HsmR","HsmA","FezA","FezB","Cbh (bile salt hydrolase)","NanR","CadC","IL-6","TNF-\u03b1","CXCL1","IL-1\u03b2","IL-36G","MUC2","Ly6G","CD11b","CD45","\u03b2-actin","S100a8","S100a9","IL36g","Cxcl1","16S rRNA","GAPDH","sCD14","LBP","Rho","Rac","Cdc42","P1B6-ATPase transporter","OppA","CotD","CotG","SodA","Fbp68","AdhR (Clostridium beijerinckii)","Bacterial \u03b2-glucuronidases","Azoreductases","Bile salt hydrolases (BSH)","Sulphatases","Nitroreductases","Cytochrome P450 system","H+/K+-ATPase","COX-1","COX-2","HMG-CoA reductase","SGLT1","SGLT2","OCT1","FXR","AMPK","Amuc_1100","TLR2","GPR41","GPR43","GLP-1","FGF19","C4","Metalloproteins","Enterobactin","Aerobactin","Yersiniabactin"],"key_pathways": ["Nutritional immunity (calprotectin/lipocalin-2 sequestration of metals)","Siderophore uptake (predicted fhu operon)","Ferrous iron import via FeoAB","Ferrosome formation (fez operon)","Heme detoxification (HatRT system)","Heme incorporation into membrane (HsmRA system)","Stickland fermentation (proline reduction)","Arginine biosynthesis and metabolism","Secondary bile acid metabolism","Spore germination pathway (taurocholate-mediated)","Sporulation pathway","Toxin production (TcdA/TcdB)","Neutrophil recruitment and inflammatory response","GTPase inactivation by TcdA/TcdB","Cytoskeleton nutrient availability)","FXR signalling (bile acid homeostasis)","Short-chain fatty acid (SCFA) signalling via GPR41/43","Glycolysis","Gluconeogenesis","Mannitol catabolism","Selenoprotein synthesis pathway","Oxidative stress defense","Iron complex transport system (M00240)","ABC-2 type transport system","Amino acid biosynthesis (histidine, inosine monophosphate)","Antibiotic resistance (acriflavine, glycylcycline, bacitracin, macrolide, multidrug)","Quorum sensing regulation of toxin synthesis","Metronidazole resistance mechanism (iron-mediated)","Sialic acid catabolism/regulation"],"key_metabolites": ["Iron (Fe2+, Fe3+)","Heme","Calprotectin","Lipocalin-2","Catecholate siderophores","Deferoxamine (DFO)","Ferrichrome","Proline","Hydroxyproline","Taurocholate","Glycocholate","Cholate","Chenodeoxycholate","Lithocholate","Deoxycholate","Tauroursodeoxycholic acid (TUDCA)","Glycoursodeoxycholic acid (GUDCA)","Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs: butyrate, acetate, propionate, lactate)","Butyrate","2-hydroxyisobutyrate","Glutamate","Sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine","Leucine","Valine","Formate","Acetate","Mannitol","Sialic acid","Dipicolonic acid","Sodium taurocholate","Metronidazole","Vancomycin","Fidaxomicin","Cobalamin (vitamin B12)","Vitamin K","Biotin","Conjugated linoleic acids","Polyphenols (tannic acids, ellagitannins, proanthocyanins, tea polyphenols)","Fructo-oligosaccharides (FOS)","Galacto-oligosaccharides (GOS)","Inulin","Lactulose","Mannose","Raffinose","\u03b2-hydroxybutyrate","Uric acid","cis-aconitate","Glycochenodeoxycholate","Trimethylamine (TMA)","Trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO)"],"biological_mechanisms": [{"mechanism": "High dietary iron (400 mg/kg ferrous sulfate) attenuates primary and recurrent CDI in mice by reducing C. difficile colonization, TcdB toxin production, and intestinal neutrophil recruitment, while decreasing pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-\u03b1, IL-1\u03b2, CXCL1, IL-36G).","evidence": "Figure1m-n (reduced bacterial loads & TcdB), Figure2a-h (reduced neutrophil %, reduced mRNA/protein of Il6, Tnfa, etc.) from source 'Dietary iron attenuates Clostridioides difficile infection...'"},{"mechanism": "High dietary iron reshapes gut microbiota, enriching Enterobacteriaceae (especially Escherichia-Shigella) and reducing microbial diversity; a specific isolate E. coli AVS0501 protects against CDI by reducing L-proline and increasing TUDCA in the gut.","evidence": "Figure3j-o (PCA/PCoA, Shannon/Ace, Enterobacteriaceae increase); Figure4b-k (E. coli AVS0501 effects); Figure6a (proline reduction); Figure7a-b (TUDCA inhibition) from same source."},{"mechanism": "L-proline promotes C. difficile growth and toxin production; proline-free diet ameliorates CDI. TUDCA directly inhibits C. difficile colonization and toxin production. High iron diet reduces intestinal proline and increases TUDCA levels.","evidence": "Figure6b-c (proline increases growth & TcdB in vitro); Figure6e-n (proline-free diet alleviates CDI); Figure7c-l (TUDCA gavage alleviates CDI) from same source."},{"mechanism": "Excess iron (Fe3+) in vitro stimulates C. difficile growth (up to13.8-fold at100 \u00b5M), upregulates tcdA/tcdB expression, and increases toxin A protein (up to8.6-fold), partially through increased cell density. Iron also reduces susceptibility to metronidazole (8-fold MIC increase) and fidaxomicin, but not vancomycin.","evidence": "Figure1a-d, Figure2a-c, Figure3b-d from source 'antibiotics-11-00537'"},{"mechanism": "Host nutritional immunity: Neutrophils release calprotectin and lipocalin-2 to sequester transition metals (Fe, Zn, Ni, Mn, Cu) and siderophores, limiting metal availability for C. difficile.","evidence": "Paper on transition metals: 'Neutrophils release cytosolic calprotectin ... sequesters divalent first row transition metals ... Lipocalin-2 interferes with bacterial Fe uptake by binding to catecholate siderophores.'"},{"mechanism": "C. difficile adaptation to iron limitation: Under low Fe, Fur derepression upregulates zupT, fhu operon (siderophore uptake), and feo genes (ferrous iron import). Under high heme, HatRT and HsmRA systems manage heme toxicity and incorporation.","evidence": "Same transition metals paper: 'Fur deepression ... zupT ... fhu operon ... feo genes' and 'Heme binding to HatR ... HsmR activates HsmA.'"},{"mechanism": "C. difficile possesses ferrosome organelles (fez operon) that store excess iron and protect against sudden iron influx and host calprotectin-mediated iron restriction. Loss of ferrosomes reduces bacterial burden and inflammation in CDI models.","evidence": "Source '1-s2.0-S1931312823005036-main': fez operon required for ferrosomes; fezB-deficient mutants show reduced colonization and inflammation."},{"mechanism": "Bile salt hydrolase (Cbh) deconjugates taurocholate to cholate, inhibiting C. difficile spore germination and vegetative growth. Engineered probiotics expressing Cbh under a sialic acid sensor protect against CDI in mice.","evidence": "Source '41467_2022_Article_31334': Purified Cbh converted99.2% taurocholate; EcN-Cbh reduced spore germination by98% and gave100% survival."},{"mechanism": "Probiotic supplementation (spore-forming probiotics) reduces intestinal inflammation (decreases TNF\u03b1, calprotectin), supports tight junction integrity, and lowers serum iron levels during recurrent CDI, suggesting modulation of nutritional immunity.","evidence": "Source 'A Spore-Forming Probiotic Supplement...': lower TNF\u03b1 and calprotectin, increased tight junction proteins, lower serum iron."},{"mechanism": "C. difficile spores germinate in response to conjugated primary bile acids (taurocholate, glycocholate); chenodeoxycholate acts as a competitive inhibitor. Fidaxomicin inhibits sporulation and outgrowth but not germination initiation.","evidence": "Source '1-s2.0-S1684118221001328-main': spore biology section; fidaxomicin effects."},{"mechanism": "Dietary iron supplementation increases colonic iron, enriching Gram-negative Enterobacteriaceae (E. coli, Klebsiella, Salmonella) via siderophore systems while depleting Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium, and elevates faecal calprotectin (intestinal inflammation marker). This dysbiosis may predispose to CDI.","evidence": "Source 'pharmaceutics-18-00651': multiple RCTs in humans show iron fortification increases Enterobacteriaceae and calprotectin, decreases Bifidobacterium."},{"mechanism": "Metagenomic analysis of CDI patients reveals enrichment of iron complex transport system (M00240) in gut microbiota, suggesting iron acquisition contributes to CDI pathogenesis. Antibiotic resistance genes (acriflavine, glycylcycline) are also enriched in CDI.","evidence": "Source 'Metagenomic analysis reveals distinct patterns...': enrichment of M00240 in CDI; also specific resistance functions."}],"comparison_design_suggestions": [{"comparison": "High-iron diet before infection (HID+CDI) vs. standard chow + CDI (CDI alone)","purpose": "To assess the direct effect of high-iron diet on CDI severity, colonization, toxin production, and immune response."},{"comparison": "CDI alone vs. healthy control (no infection, standard diet)","purpose": "To establish baseline CDI phenotype and changes in microbiota, metabolites, and immune markers."},{"comparison": "High-iron diet before infection vs. healthy control","purpose": "To evaluate the effect of high-iron diet alone (without infection) on gut microbiota composition and baseline immune parameters."},{"comparison": "FMT from high-iron diet donors + CDI vs. FMT from control donors + CDI","purpose": "To test the causal role of high-iron-induced microbiota in protection against CDI."},{"comparison": "E. coli AVS0501 treatment + CDI vs. vehicle + CDI","purpose": "To validate the protective effect of the key bacterial strain isolated from high-iron fed mice."},{"comparison": "Proline-free diet + CDI vs. normal diet + CDI","purpose": "To determine the contribution of L-proline to CDI susceptibility."},{"comparison": "TUDCA supplementation + CDI vs. vehicle + CDI","purpose": "To test the therapeutic effect of TUDCA on CDI severity."},{"comparison": "High-iron diet + iron chelator (e.g., DFO) + CDI vs. high-iron diet + CDI","purpose": "To confirm that the iron content is responsible for the observed effects."}],"expected_findings": ["High dietary iron (400 mg/kg ferrous sulfate) attenuates both primary and recurrent CDI in mice.","High-iron diet reduces C. difficile colonization and TcdB toxin levels in cecal contents and feces.","High-iron diet decreases intestinal neutrophil recruitment and lowers pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-\u03b1, IL-1\u03b2, CXCL1, IL-36G).","High-iron diet alters gut microbiota: enriches Enterobacteriaceae (especially Escherichia/Shigella), reduces microbial diversity.","A specific E. coli strain (AVS0501) isolated from high-iron fed mice protects against CDI by reducing L-proline and increasing TUDCA.","L-proline promotes C. difficile growth and toxin production; proline-free diet reduces CDI severity.","TUDCA inhibits C. difficile growth and toxin; oral TUDCA alleviates CDI.","Fecal microbiota transplantation from high-iron fed mice partially recapitulates protection.","Contrast with in vitro findings: excess iron directly enhances C. difficile growth and toxin expression, but in vivo dietary iron may act through microbiota and metabolite modulation to reduce net pathogenicity.","Iron supplementation (oral) in humans consistently enriches Enterobacteriaceae and increases intestinal inflammation (calprotectin), which could increase risk for CDI in certain contexts.","C. difficile utilizes multiple iron acquisition and storage systems (Feo, fhu, fez operons) that are important for virulence and survival in the iron-limited host environment."],"references": [{"title": "Transition metals and Clostridioides difficile infection (Trends in Microbiology review)","key_finding": "Host nutritional immunity via calprotectin/lipocalin-2; C. difficile adaptation to metal limitation (Fur, Feo, ZupT, heme detoxification); iron fluctuations alter microbiota."},{"title": "C. difficile spore biology and recurrence mechanisms","key_finding": "Spore germination by taurocholate, inhibition by chenodeoxycholate; fidaxomicin inhibits sporulation/outgrowth; vancomycin-loaded nanoparticles delay germination."},{"title": "Ferrosomes: iron storage organelles in C. difficile (Cell Host & Microbe)","key_finding": "fez operon encodes ferrosomes; protects against iron toxicity and calprotectin; fezB mutant attenuates CDI in mice."},{"title": "Gut microbiome resistance mechanisms and therapeutic strategies for CDI (review)","key_finding": "Probiotics, prebiotics, synbiotics, FMT, postbiotics inhibit C. difficile via competitive exclusion, antimicrobials, immune stimulation, microenvironment modulation."},{"title": "Engineered probiotics for CDI via bile salt hydrolase and sialic acid sensor (Nature Communications)","key_finding": "Cbh deconjugates taurocholate to cholate, inhibiting spore germination; EcN-Cbh protects mice (100% survival) and restores bile salt metabolism."},{"title": "Probiotic supplement improves intestinal immune response during recurrent CDI in mice","key_finding": "Probiotics reduce TNF\u03b1, calprotectin, support tight junctions; lower serum iron levels suggest modulation of nutritional immunity."},{"title": "Iron stimulates C. difficile growth, toxin production, and metronidazole resistance (Antibiotics)","key_finding": "Fe3+ increases growth (13.8-fold), toxin A (8.6-fold), metronidazole MIC (8-fold); deferoxamine reverses these effects."},{"title": "Dietary iron attenuates CDI via modulation of immune response and gut microbiota (main source)","key_finding": "High-iron diet (400 mg/kg) reduces CDI severity, alters microbiota (enriches E. coli AVS0501), reduces proline, increases TUDCA, reduces neutrophil infiltration."},{"title": "Probiotic cocktail (Pro11) protects against CDI via microbiota and metabolome modulation","key_finding": "Pro11 reduces C. difficile burden and toxin, strengthens gut barrier, increases butyrate, enriches Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium, decreases Enterococcus."},{"title": "Metagenomic analysis of gut microbiota in CDI, asymptomatic carriage, and non-CDI diarrhea","key_finding": "Iron complex transport system enriched in CDI microbiota; specific species (Enterobacteriaceae, Klebsiella) enriched; antibiotic resistance functions differ."},{"title": "Effects of common medications (PPIs, metformin, NSAIDs, statins, SGLT2i, oral iron) on gut microbiota (Pharmaceutics review)","key_finding": "Oral iron enriches Enterobacteriaceae, depletes Lactobacillus/Bifidobacterium, increases calprotectin; PPIs increase CDI risk (OR1.99)."}]}

# Previous Module Conclusions


# Preprocessing Plan
Expression Matrix Preprocessing

plan goal: Preprocess the metabolomics expression matrix (2059 metabolites ×18 samples) by handling missing values, normalizing to relative abundance, assessing log-transformation needs, and performing median scaling per molecule, to prepare data for downstream differential analysis and biomarker discovery related to high-iron diet effects on Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI).
plan notes: 1. DATA TYPE: This is untargeted metabolomics LC-MS data (2059 metabolites). The expression values range ~0.1 to ~5.8, which is relatively narrow. The standard preprocessing pipeline will be applied to ensure consistency.

2. QC SAMPLES: Three QC samples (Fc16YH_QC01-03) are listed in sampleinfo.csv but do NOT appear in the expression matrix columns — they are excluded.

3. SAMPLE GROUPS:
 - 'high iron diet before' (FE1-FE6): High-iron diet + CDI infection (experimental)
 - 'Clostridium difficile infection' (CD1-CD6): CDI infection only (disease control)
 - 'Standard (control)' (NC1-NC6): Healthy, no infection (baseline control)

4. LOG TRANSFORMATION SKIPPED: Raw max value (~5.8) <<100, indicating the data is already on a linear scale.

5. MOLECULE ANNOTATION: A complementary file (G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/metabolites.csv) with2172 entries and32 annotation columns is available for downstream biological interpretation.

6. RESEARCH CONTEXT: Preprocessed data will be used to compare metabolite abundance across groups to identify metabolites mediating the protective effect of high-iron diet against CDI, particularly L-proline, TUDCA, bile acids, and SCFAs as described in the research background.

# Your Task
Write a complete R script that:

Read expression matrix and sample metadata. Set the first unnamed column (empty header) as the molecule/compound identifier (row names). Separate the18 sample columns (Fc16YH_CD1-6, Fc16YH_FE1-6, Fc16YH_NC1-6) from the molecule names. Note that3 QC samples (Fc16YH_QC01-03) exist in sampleinfo.csv but are absent from the expression matrix — they will be ignored during preprocessing.
Load and parse the raw expression data correctly, establish molecule-to-sample structure.

All scripts and the generated CSV files are placed in this designated temporary workspace folder: G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/demo/tmp/1_expression_matrix_preprocessing
All pdf/png figure image files should save to workspace folder: G:/OmicsWorks/test/metabolism/demo/analysis/1_expression_matrix_preprocessing/figure
